one_line_takeaway: SSP trains a single LLM to alternately attack and defend itself in an RL self-play loop with UCB-prioritized replay of past failure cases, cutting attack success rate substantially below static-red-team baselines (e.g., GCG ASR 85.2% -> 1.7% on Qwen2.5-7B) while mostly preserving task capability and reducing over-refusal versus compared defenses.
key_finding: On Table 1 (ASR %, lower=better) SSP achieves the lowest or near-lowest ASR across nearly all attack x model combinations, e.g. on Qwen2.5-7B: GCG 1.7 vs No-Defense 85.2 and next-best ACE-safety 2.5; PAIR 2.4 vs ACE-safety 3.1; TAP 1.4 vs ACE-safety 2.9; on Llama3-8B: DAN 1.5 vs ACE-safety 5.2; on Vicuna-7B SSP is not uniformly best (e.g. GCG 8.8 vs ACE-safety 8.5, PAIR 6.7 vs ACE-safety 9.8). Table 2 shows SSP largely preserves capability versus Vanilla, e.g. Qwen2.5-7B GSM8K 87.2 (Vanilla 91.6, SafeDecoding 82.4, ACE-Safety 83.1) and MBPP 75.6 (Vanilla 79.2). Table 3 shows SSP has the lowest over-refusal rate on OR-Bench among compared defenses (Qwen2.5-7B 25.3% vs ACE-Safety 29.6%, SafeDecoding 30.1%, SmoothLLM 34.5%, Self-Reminder 36.2%). Table 4 ablation shows removing the unified attacker/defender model (w/o UM), the replay mechanism (w/o Replay), or UCB specifically all degrade ASR relative to full SSP (e.g. Qwen2.5 GCG: w/o UM 5.8, w/o Replay 4.7, w/o UCB 3.8, vs full SSP 1.7), confirming each component contributes. Table 5 (attacker-role evaluation on HarmBench, ASR and self-BLEU diversity DIV) shows SSP's attacker matches or exceeds strong dedicated attack baselines' diversity without an explicit diversity reward, e.g. on Qwen3-8B target SSP ASR 84.50/DIV 0.964 vs Jailbreak-R1 83.00/0.959, though on Vicuna-7B and Llama3-8B targets SSP's ASR is close to but slightly below Jailbreak-R1 (83.50 vs 85.00; 52.50 vs 54.00).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Safety Self-Play (SSP): a single LLM policy that is trained via RL to simultaneously play Attacker (generating jailbreak prompts for a harmful goal G) and Defender (producing a response to that prompt), scored by an external LLM-based safety judge on a discrete 1-5 scale that is converted into a zero-sum reward pair r_att = clip((Score-1)/4) and r_def = 1 - r_att = clip((5-Score)/4). This removes the need for a separate, static external red-team attacker. The second original component is a 'Reflective Experience Replay Mechanism': failure cases (goals the Attacker couldn't jailbreak with, or prompts the Defender couldn't refuse) below difficulty thresholds tau_att/tau_def are stored in two pools P_att/P_def, and replayed using an Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) sampling rule UCB_i = (1 - r_i) + c*sqrt(ln N / (n_i+1)) that favors low-reward, low-sample-count items; items are re-scored on replay and evicted once their reward exceeds a threshold tau. The two mechanisms are combined into a single RL objective (Eq. 6/7) trained with a shared policy theta.
threat_model: Adversary/attacker is the model's own policy acting in the Attacker role, generating adversarial jailbreak prompts pattack for a harmful goal G; the Defender role of the same policy must refuse. Safety is judged post-hoc by an external LLM-based judge instantiated with the target model's prohibited-usage guidelines, scoring responses 1 (proactive refusal) to 5 (complete safety failure fulfilling the harmful request) along two axes: alignment with policy-violating intent and severity of violation. Evaluation-time threat model covers external jailbreak attack families applied against the trained defender: optimization-based (GCG, SAA/simple adaptive attacks), LLM-driven iterative attackers (PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN-Turbo, AdvPrompt, GPO, ArrAttack, Jailbreak-R1), and prompt-template attacks (DAN, DeepInception/DI), run on benchmarks derived from HarmBench and AdvBench, with training goals (5,000 harmful goals from Jailbreak-R1's aggregated dataset) filtered out of the test set to avoid leakage.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) attack diversity is still bounded by the initial harmful-goal set and the base model's inherent creative range; (2) the current implementation is text-only and does not cover multimodal (image/audio/video) jailbreak threats; (3) the iterative self-play RL process adds training cost relative to standard SFT, and more resource-efficient variants are left to future work; (4) long-term stability against future, unseen attack techniques is not established by the paper's evaluation and requires further longitudinal study. Additional grounding-agent observations: only the main text and first ~22 pages were extracted (References section runs long and Appendices A/B, referenced for detailed configs and ablation settings, were not fully inspected in this pass); the paper does not specify exactly how the six 'victim models' (including GPT-4o and Gemini-3.0-fast) interact with the trained 7B/8B backbones in the main-result tables, since Table 1's models are Qwen2.5-7B/Vicuna-7B/Llama3-8B/Mistral3-8B only.

datasets_used: Jailbreak-R1 training set: 5,000 harmful goals aggregated from Shaikh et al. 2023, Bhardwaj et al. 2024, Mazeika et al. 2024 (HarmBench), Dai et al. 2023
HarmBench (evaluation benchmark for jailbreak attacks and attacker-capability analysis, Table 5)
quantitative_results: Table 1 ASR (%): Llama3-8B No-Defense DAN 82.1 -> SSP 1.5; GCG 78.4 -> 1.5; SAA 84.6 -> 3.5
Table 1 ASR (%): Vicuna-7B SSP not always best -- GCG 8.8 (vs ACE-safety 8.5), DI 3.4 (vs CircuitBreakers 3.5)
Table 2 Qwen2.5-7B: Vanilla GSM8K 91.6 / MBPP 79.2 / MMLU 79.5 vs SSP GSM8K 87.2 / MBPP 75.6 / MMLU 72.6

Table 3 OR-Bench refusal rate (%): SSP 25.3 (Qwen2.5-7B), 24.6 (LLaMA3-8B), 24.1 (Mistral3-8B) -- lowest among Self-Reminder (36.2/35.1/34.5), SmoothLLM (34.5/33.2/32.6), SafeDecoding (30.1/29.3/28.7), ACE-Safety (29.6/28.7/28.0)
Table 4 ablation, Qwen2.5 GCG ASR: w/o UM 5.8, w/o Replay 4.7, w/o UCB 3.8, full SSP 1.7
Table 5 HarmBench attacker-role: SSP vs Jailbreak-R1 ASR/DIV on Qwen3-8B target 84.50/0.964 vs 83.00/0.959; on Vicuna-7B target 83.50/0.957 vs 85.00/0.954; on GPT-4o target 59.00/0.971 vs 57.50/0.973
baselines_compared: Attack baselines for attacker-role eval: AdvPrompt, TAP, AutoDAN, PAIR, GPO, AutoDAN-Turbo, ArrAttack, Jailbreak-R1
